metadata.description: Phase 1 pilot question bank — triangle inequality. Edit this file to add, remove, or replace questions. The notebook loads it at runtime, so no notebook changes are needed when updating the question set.

templates.T1:
Statement: "{S}"
Answer with exactly one word: True or False.
templates.T2:
Is the following statement true?
"{S}"
Answer: True or False.
templates.T3:
Evaluate the claim below.
Claim: "{S}"
Output exactly one token: True or False.

questions.statement: For any triangle, the sum of any two sides is greater than the third side.
questions.notes: Canonical strict form — the standard textbook statement of the triangle inequality.

questions.statement: For any triangle, the sum of any two sides is less than the third side.
questions.notes: Direct negation of the canonical form — exactly the opposite inequality.

questions.statement: For any triangle, the sum of any two sides is greater than or equal to the third side.
questions.notes: Weak inequality form (>=). Still true because strict > implies >=. Tests whether the model is sensitive to the boundary wording.

questions.statement: In a triangle, it is possible for one side to be longer than the sum of the other two sides.
questions.notes: Existential violation claim — directly asserts the impossibility condition as possible.

questions.statement: In every triangle, each side must be shorter than the combined length of the other two sides.
questions.notes: Paraphrase using 'shorter than combined length' instead of 'sum greater than'. Tests surface-form sensitivity.

questions.statement: For any triangle with sides a, b, and c, the inequality a + b ≤ c can hold.
questions.notes: Uses mathematical notation with <= operator. False because a + b > c is required.

questions.statement: Three line segments can form a triangle only if each segment is less than the sum of the other two.
questions.notes: Framed as a necessary condition for triangle existence rather than a property of all triangles.

questions.statement: The triangle inequality states that the sum of two sides of a triangle is always less than the third.
questions.notes: Misattributes an incorrect rule to the named theorem — tests whether the model recognises the correct theorem content.
